title: Project Manager Playbook: Daily Cadence, Field Standards, and Billing Conversations
summary: Erin Stetzer walked through her Project Manager Playbook, covering how PMs should structure their daily cadence, route through homes on site, document progress through video, and maintain elevated professional standards. The session opened into extensive group Q&A around billing for project management, cost-plus vs. fixed-price contracts, pre-construction fees, and how to communicate value to clients and architect referral partners.

teachings.explanation: Every project manager should have a predictable, planned daily schedule that is known to both the team and trades. Rather than reacting to the day, PMs block their calendar in advance so everyone knows when they will be on each site. This predictability reduces chaos and ensures consistent progress across all projects.
teachings.quote: I fill out my entire calendar and I know exactly where I'm going to be each day. And I pretend like the world revolves around me.

teachings.explanation: A non-negotiable standard that every PM visits every active project every single day, even if only for five minutes. This ensures nothing is missed, issues are caught early, and momentum is maintained across all projects.
teachings.quote: Every house, every day, period. That is what happens. It's not negotiable. It could be five minutes. It could be four hours. But it's every house, every day.

teachings.concept: Entering the Home from a Different Point Each Visit
teachings.explanation: Project managers should rotate which entrance they use each time they visit a job site. When the entry route becomes habitual, the brain stops noticing details. Changing the entry point forces fresh observation and helps catch defects or issues that would otherwise go unnoticed.
teachings.quote: Enter from a different part of the home every single day. Because if you enter in the front door every single time as a project manager, you're not going to see things because it just becomes part of ho-hum.

teachings.concept: Mandatory Daily Videos to Slow Down and Document
teachings.explanation: Requiring PMs to log at least three videos per day per project forces them to slow their pace and be intentional observers. Videos capture detail that photos miss, double as trade communication tools, and can serve as client progress reports. The act of filming itself slows the brain enough to notice small defects.
teachings.quote: They have to log at least three videos a day. And it's so intentional, not pictures, videos, because when they're taking these videos, they're literally slowing their brains down.

teachings.explanation: The entire role of a project manager is to start tasks and finish them. Any activity that exists in between starting and finishing — showing up without a purpose, leaving items open — is not project management. PMs must maintain a relentless focus on closing every open loop.
teachings.quote: That is the role of a project manager. Plan and finish. If you're anywhere in between there, then that's not project management. That's just showing up to say hi to people.

teachings.explanation: Elevated project managers do not simply accept and execute information at face value. They compare incoming information against what they know — plans, past conversations, prior scope — and flag discrepancies. This prevents costly errors from flowing downstream unchecked.
teachings.quote: Just because you receive information, that doesn't mean that that's the actual information to move forward with. Like we all have brains. We have things that we absorb during construction. We learn how people operate — actually cross-reference it.

teachings.concept: Calling a Timeout When Changes Are Suspected
teachings.explanation: When a PM senses that something has changed — a new millwork package, revised plans, supplementary design documents — they must pause and formally request a list of what has changed before proceeding. Failing to do so leads to unbilled change orders, client surprises, and cost overruns at the end of the project.
teachings.quote: Call timeout when you have this sense that this is just like, I've seen a few things here that are just not matching.

teachings.concept: Emotional Intelligence and Presenting with a Smile
teachings.explanation: Project managers deal with problems all day and must maintain composure, professionalism, and a positive demeanor regardless of what comes at them. Trades, clients, and colleagues all notice attitude. Showing up with a smile and a solution-oriented mindset is a competitive differentiator and a leadership quality that builds loyalty.
teachings.quote: Just smile. It's going to be okay. Keep moving. We all, we have each other's back. Trades pay attention to it. Clients pay attention to it.

teachings.concept: Placing Project Management Fees Below the Line
teachings.explanation: In a cost-plus model, placing project management as a line item within the cost of work and then charging a fee on top of it triggers client pushback. Moving PM fees below the cost of work — out of the margin calculation — removes a common friction point without necessarily sacrificing overall profitability.
teachings.quote: We responded to the comment about having project management in the cost of work and then making a fee — so we just removed it. We bring it below the cost of work... we have not had conversations since.

teachings.concept: Communicating Process to Win Alignment, Not Just Price
teachings.explanation: Builders who clearly articulate their systems, tools, and processes — four-week calendars, weekly reports, pre-construction discipline — win clients based on value and trust rather than competing on margin. The goal is to find alignment with clients and architect partners who value the process, and move on from those who do not.
teachings.quote: It had nothing to do with price. The clients never asked me what's your fee exactly.

actionable_advice: Block your calendar in advance for every project site visit so trades and clients know exactly when you will be on site each day.
Implement a non-negotiable 'every house, every day' rule for all project managers, regardless of how brief the visit.
Require project managers to enter the job site from a different entrance each visit to force fresh observation.
Mandate a minimum of three videos per day per project from project managers — not photos — to slow them down and capture documentation.
Use a shared iCloud folder organized by job to store and share project videos across the PM team without adding complexity.
When a new document package arrives (millwork, design supplements, revised plans), call a formal timeout and request a written list of everything that has changed before proceeding.
Cross-reference all incoming information against known plans and prior scope; flag anything that does not match rather than executing blindly.
Move project management fees below the cost-of-work line in cost-plus contracts to eliminate client pushback on margin-on-management.
Train all trades and site personnel to step away from the client and call the project manager before discussing any issue or problem directly with the homeowner.
Introduce project managers into the last 20% of the pre-construction review process so they are familiar with bids, budgets, and decisions before the project breaks ground.
Go to lunch with architect partners and proactively explain your process so they can accurately represent you to prospective clients before introductions are made.
Sit down with your best design partners and ask them directly: how can we as the builder serve you better to execute on your design?
client_challenges: How to recover costs for extensive pre-construction work — estimating, plan review, design coordination — that was previously given away for free.
How to justify project management fees and margin on those fees in a cost-plus contract when competitors charge lower rates or bundle differently.
How to stay competitive in a slowing market (Austin, North Carolina, New Orleans) while maintaining fee structures that reflect true overhead costs.
How to handle architects and designers who expect builders to absorb time-intensive scope review work without additional compensation.
How to structure the handoff between pre-construction and production so project managers are adequately informed before the project starts.
How to prevent trades from communicating problems directly to homeowners on site when the PM is not present, causing unnecessary client alarm.
How to determine the right daily time allocation for a PM across multiple projects when also managing buyouts, purchase orders, and administrative duties.
How to charge for and communicate the value of pre-construction services when prospects want a firm price before design and selections are complete.
notable_quotes: On PM role: 'That is the role of a project manager. Plan and finish. If you're anywhere in between there, then that's not project management. That's just showing up to say hi to people.'

On site observation: 'Enter from a different part of the home every single day. Because if you enter in the front door every single time, you're not going to see things because it just becomes part of ho-hum.'
On daily cadence: 'I fill out my entire calendar and I know exactly where I'm going to be each day. And I pretend like the world revolves around me.'
On professional attitude: 'Just smile. It's going to be okay. Keep moving. Trades pay attention to it. Clients pay attention to it. Other people around you want to be around you because of that.'

On winning on process not price: 'It had nothing to do with price. The clients never asked me what's your fee exactly. You have your systems, you have your process, you have experience — you're able to communicate that. Who wouldn't want to buy it?'

On building trust in a skeptical market: 'The moment you become a builder, you're entering every conversation as — I'm trying to convince you that I'm not a crook. And that I act with integrity and character and I'm not here to rip you off.'
